items.q: Why can a profitable business run out of cash?
items.correct: Because profit counts credit sales and ignores timing — if customers pay slowly while wages, rent and suppliers demand cash now, the firm can become insolvent despite profits.
items.misconception: Profitable businesses always have enough cash.
items.random_wrong: Cash shortages only happen to charities.
items.why: Profit-versus-cash-flow is the single most examined finance misconception at SL.

items.q: Is break-even the point where a business becomes successful?
items.correct: It is only where total revenue equals total costs — no profit, no loss; success depends on how far sales exceed it (the margin of safety) and on wider objectives.
items.misconception: Reaching break-even means the business is now profitable.
items.random_wrong: Break-even is when machines break down evenly.
items.why: Students attach success language to break-even instead of treating it as a threshold.

items.q: Is revenue the same as profit?
items.correct: No — revenue is income from sales (price times quantity); profit is what remains after all costs are subtracted, so high revenue can coexist with losses.
items.misconception: A business with millions in sales must be making millions in profit.
items.random_wrong: Revenue is the money owed to the tax office.
items.why: The most basic and persistent finance confusion, worth securing early.

items.q: Luca Pacioli's 'Summa de Arithmetica' detailing double-entry bookkeeping dates from:
items.why: Correct answer: 1494. “1390” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Net present value. “Payback” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Year Pacioli published 'Summa' codifying double-entry bookkeeping?
items.why: The real figure is 1494 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
